input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) characterized by the need for **multi-level abstraction modeling** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key components include **physical objects** (e.g., machinery, equipment, or assets) whose state is monitored via **automated one-way data flows** (as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model). The operational domain spans **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives**, requiring integration of diverse modeling notations (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and tools. The system’s suitability for digital twinning stems from its **dynamic, interconnected nature**, where **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor data, operational logs) are aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes (e.g., predictive maintenance, process optimization). The reference model (Fig. 1) emphasizes **UML-based class diagrams** to formalize digital shadow concepts, implying the system’s reliance on **structured, object-oriented modeling** for digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides **active services** to users and the physical system, contrasting with the passive **Digital Shadow** (which only collects data). Key services include:
  1. **Data Interpretation & Processing**: Active components (e.g., software agents, rule engines) interpret incoming **one-way data flows** from the physical system to derive actionable insights.
  2. **Behavioral Modeling**: Integration with **state machines** (e.g., SysML v1.7) or **automata** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton) to simulate and predict system behavior dynamically.
  3. **Simulation & Optimization**: Use of **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation engines) to model and optimize system performance, including **predictive analytics** for failure prevention or process improvements.
  4. **Visualization & Decision Support**: Generation of **multi-perspective views** (e.g., functional, logical, or technical architectures) to support stakeholders (engineers, operators, managers) in monitoring and controlling the system.
  5. **Autonomous Control**: Potential for **closed-loop control** (implied by DT’s active role) where the digital twin influences the physical system via feedback mechanisms, though specifics are not detailed in the document.
  Target users include **system engineers, operators, and decision-makers** requiring real-time or near-real-time insights.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the Digital Twin leverages a **heterogeneous toolchain** combining:
  1. **Modeling Languages & Frameworks**:
     - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**, covering requirements, functional/logical architectures, and product layers. Includes **Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** for component-connector views and **state machines** (v1.7+) for behavioral modeling.
     - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc** (with **MontiArc Automaton** for behavioral modeling) to describe **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**.
  2. **Simulation & Execution Engines**:
     - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **detailed behavioral simulation** and dynamic system analysis.
     - **General-purpose languages**: **C++/Java** for implementing custom logic or integrating with physical systems.
     - **Other simulation tools/methods** (e.g., [29], [30]) for domain-specific modeling (e.g., physics-based simulations).
  3. **Integration & Data Management**:
     - **UML Class Diagrams**: Used in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1)** to formalize data aggregation and abstraction workflows.
     - **Heterogeneous tool integration**: Required to handle **multi-notation models** (e.g., combining SysML with ADLs or simulation tools) and **stakeholder-specific perspectives**.
  4. **Development Methodologies**:
     - **MBSE methodologies**: Such as **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** to manage complexity across abstraction layers.
     - **Component-based development**: Emphasized by ADLs to modularize system design and facilitate reuse.
  The toolchain supports **end-to-end digital twin lifecycle**, from **data collection (digital shadow)** to **active service provision (digital twin)** via modeling, simulation, and execution.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly detail multiplicities or hierarchical structures of Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, it implies a **centralized vs. decentralized architecture** through the use of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc, which may support distributed or modular DT deployments. The document also references **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-perspective modeling**, suggesting potential coordination mechanisms (e.g., via **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** or **MontiArc Automaton**) for managing responsibilities across DT instances. No explicit mention of synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** is implicitly addressed through the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) and the broader MBSE framework. Orchestration and coordination are enabled by **architecture description languages (ADLs)** (e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc) and **behavioral modeling tools** (e.g., SysML state machines, MathWorks Simulink, or general-purpose code in C++/Java). The document emphasizes **component integration** via **interfaces, connectors, and composition** (e.g., SysML IBDs) and **resource management** through hierarchical abstraction layers (requirements → functions → logics → physics). System-wide coordination may leverage **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., simulation tools like Simulink or other modeling languages) to ensure consistency across physical and digital representations. No explicit architecture patterns (e.g., microservices, service-oriented) are mentioned, but the modularity of ADLs suggests potential for scalable orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The document highlights **horizontal integration** through **interoperability standards** and **data exchange protocols** implied by MBSE methodologies. Specifically, **SysML** and **ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc)** facilitate integration with external systems via **standardized interfaces and connectors**, enabling communication between DT components and enterprise systems. The use of **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)** and **simulation tools (Simulink)** suggests dependencies on external systems for behavioral modeling and validation. While no explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are mentioned, the **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., combining SysML with Simulink) implies a need for **interoperability frameworks** to bridge disparate systems. The document does not specify integration patterns with other Digital Twins but references **multi-perspective modeling** (e.g., for stakeholders), which may extend to cross-DT coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key modeling approaches include **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for structural and behavioral descriptions, with **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc (including MontiArc Automaton for behavioral modeling), and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to define **component-connector architectures**, specifying system components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral models are further detailed using **simulation tools** like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++/Java**), enabling dynamic system analysis. The DT constellation integrates **contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor data, operational logs) aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, with **data management approaches** relying on tool-specific workflows to ensure consistency across heterogeneous modeling notations and stakeholder perspectives. Model relationships are established through **compositional hierarchies** and **interface-based interactions**, while data sources may include **real-time sensor feeds, historical records, and external system APIs** to populate the digital representation. The system’s fidelity depends on the granularity of these models, with **validation and verification** processes implicitly required to ensure alignment between digital and physical system states.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity considerations** through the use of **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) to define **model abstraction layers** and ensure traceability from requirements to implementation. **Validation and verification (V&V)** are implicitly addressed via **SysML-based behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7) and **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) to assess model accuracy against physical system behavior. Uncertainty sources may arise from **data abstraction gaps** between heterogeneous modeling tools or **incomplete sensor data traces**, requiring **error detection mechanisms** (e.g., anomaly detection in aggregated data streams) and **quality assurance processes** to maintain model validity. The system’s **digital shadow reference model** (as per Kritzinger et al.) emphasizes **one-way data flow** from physical to digital, necessitating **active components** (e.g., DT-specific software) to interpret and process incoming data for fidelity. However, explicit **quantification of uncertainty** or **formal error correction protocols** is not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined in the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]. This flow involves the **collection of contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational states, environmental parameters) from the physical system, followed by **aggregation, abstraction, and preprocessing** to ensure relevance and consistency. The data types transmitted may include structured (e.g., time-series sensor data, log files) and unstructured (e.g., images, audio) formats, depending on the application domain (e.g., manufacturing). Communication protocols are not explicitly specified in the document, but typical industrial implementations may leverage **OPC UA, MQTT, or AMQP** for real-time data transmission. Transmission frequencies are application-dependent but often **event-triggered** (e.g., state changes, threshold breaches) or **time-based** (e.g., periodic polling). Data quality assurance mechanisms may include **validation checks, filtering, and normalization** to handle noise or inconsistencies. Preprocessing steps could involve **feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, or anomaly detection** before integration into the digital model. Data integration mechanisms may rely on **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines** or middleware solutions to unify heterogeneous data sources into a cohesive digital representation [31].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the broader context of Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and Digital Shadow Reference Model implies the need for **reliable, low-latency communication** between physical and digital components. While specific protocols (e.g., **OPC UA, MQTT, or HTTP/REST**) are not mentioned, the infrastructure must support **heterogeneous data exchange** across stakeholders and tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink, or general-purpose languages like C++/Java). Network topology and architecture would likely follow **hybrid cloud-edge-on-premise models** to balance real-time processing and scalability. Bandwidth and latency requirements depend on the application but may prioritize **deterministic communication** for critical control flows. Redundancy mechanisms (e.g., **failover protocols, data replication**) and security protocols (e.g., **encryption, authentication via OAuth2, role-based access control**) are implied for robustness and compliance, though not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the MBSE context suggests a **multi-layered deployment model** supporting **cloud (scalable, centralized), edge (low-latency, localized), and on-premise (secure, isolated)** environments. Computational requirements would depend on the complexity of simulations (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink, custom C++/Java applications**) and real-time analytics, while storage needs may involve **structured databases (e.g., SQL) for metadata and unstructured storage (e.g., NoSQL, object storage) for raw data traces**. Scalability mechanisms would likely include **containerization (Docker), orchestration (Kubernetes), and auto-scaling** to handle dynamic workloads. Deployment models may support **hybrid or modular architectures** to integrate heterogeneous tools (e.g., SysML for design, simulation tools for behavior modeling). Availability and reliability assurances would rely on **redundant infrastructure, backup systems, and service-level agreements (SLAs)** tailored to the DT’s criticality, though specifics are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) engineering and development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition, with extensions like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines** for behavioral modeling. Behavioral details are further refined using tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]), or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**). The process integrates **heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives** and **multi-notation modeling tools**, requiring governance across diverse representations. **Quality assurance** and **validation** are implied through structured modeling and simulation, though explicit testing/validation methodologies are not detailed. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly described but are inferred as necessary for maintaining consistency across iterative model refinements and tool integrations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The provided documents reference **cybersecurity protocols** and **safety-critical system considerations** implicitly through the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (referenced in [46], [47], [50]) and **RAMI4.0** (referenced in [48], [49]). Specifically:
  - **Access Control Mechanisms**: The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** (as per [46], [47], [50]) defines a standardized structure for digital twins, including **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **authentication systems** via **OAuth 2.0** and **JWT (JSON Web Tokens)** for secure communication between components. The **AAS specification** (Part 1, [50]) mandates **encryption of data in transit** (TLS 1.2+) and **data integrity checks** via digital signatures.
  - **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: The **RAMI4.0 reference architecture** ([48], [49]) emphasizes **cybersecurity layers** across the **automation pyramid**, integrating **intrusion detection systems (IDS)** and **firewall segmentation** to isolate critical assets (e.g., PLCs, robots like the **MABI Max-100-2-25-P** in [44]). **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** approaches ([43]) leverage **formal verification** of digital twin models to preemptively identify vulnerabilities in system behavior.
  - **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: The **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** ([51]) for simulation model exchange includes **fault injection testing** to validate fail-safe behaviors in digital twins, ensuring compliance with **IEC 61508** (functional safety) standards. **MQTT-based communication** ([52]) in IoT contexts (e.g., ThingsBoard [53]) employs **QoS levels (0–2)** to prioritize critical data transmission, with **QoS 2** enabling guaranteed delivery for safety-relevant messages.
  - **Risk Assessment Approaches**: The **AAS** ([46], [50]) incorporates **risk assessment models** aligned with **ISO/IEC 27001** and **NIST SP 800-53**, requiring digital twins to document **asset lifecycle risks** (e.g., data corruption, unauthorized access) and **mitigation strategies** via **security policies** embedded in the shell’s metadata.
  - **Safety-Critical Systems**: The **MABI robotic arm** ([44]) and **friction modeling for robot dynamics** ([45]) highlight the need for **real-time safety monitors** (e.g., torque/force sensors) and **emergency stop protocols** (ISO 10218-1). The **Digital Shadow** concept ([42]) applies **predictive maintenance algorithms** to preempt failures in safety-critical components, reducing downtime risks in automated systems.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

[41] M. Konersmann, J. Michael, and B. Rumpe, “Towards Reference Models
with Conformance Relations for Structure,” in _Informing Possible Future_
_Worlds. Essays in Honour of Ulrich Frank_ . Logos Verlag Berlin, 2024,
pp. 247–269.

[42] M. Heithoff, C. Hopmann, T. Köbel, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, and P. Sapel,
“Application of Digital Shadows on Different Levels in the Automation
Pyramid,” _Data and Knowledge Engineering (DKE)_, vol. 158, 2025.

[43] M. Heithoff, J. Michael, B. Rumpe, J. Pfeiffer, A. Wortmann, and
J. Zhang, “A Method for Model-Driven Engineering of Digital Twins
in Manufacturing,” in _28th ACM/IEEE Int. Conf. on Model Driven_
_Engineering Languages and Systems_ . IEEE, 2025.

[44] MABI, “Mabi robot,” [https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)
[robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p, 2025, accessed: 2025-07-04.](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)

[45] M. Trinh, R. Schwiedernoch, L. Gründel, S. Storms, and C. Brecher,
“Friction modeling for structured learning of robot dynamics,” in _Lecture_
_Notes in Production Engineering_ . Springer, 2023, pp. 396–406.
